## **DPM-Solver: A Fast ODE Solver for Diffusion Probabilistic Model Sampling in Around 10 Steps**

**Cheng Lu** _[†]_ **, Yuhao Zhou** _[†]_ **, Fan Bao** _[†]_ **, Jianfei Chen** _[†∗]_ **, Chongxuan Li** _[‡]_ **, Jun Zhu** _[†∗]_

> _†_ Dept. of Comp. Sci. & Tech., Institute for AI, BNRist Center, THBI Lab

> _†_ Tsinghua-Bosch Joint ML Center, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084 China

> _‡_ Gaoling School of Artificial Intelligence, Renmin University of China,

> _‡_ Beijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Management and Analysis Methods, Beijing, China `{lucheng.lc15, yuhaoz.cs}@gmail.com` ; `bf19@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn chongxuanli@ruc.edu.cn` ; `{jianfeic, dcszj}@tsinghua.edu.cn`

## **Abstract**

Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) are emerging powerful generative models. Despite their high-quality generation performance, DPMs still suffer from their slow sampling as they generally need hundreds or thousands of sequential function evaluations (steps) of large neural networks to draw a sample. Sampling from DPMs can be viewed alternatively as solving the corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In this work, we propose an exact formulation of the solution of diffusion ODEs. The formulation analytically computes the linear part of the solution, rather than leaving all terms to black-box ODE solvers as adopted in previous works. By applying change-of-variable, the solution can be equivalently simplified to an exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Based on our formulation, we propose _DPM-Solver_ , a fast dedicated high-order solver for diffusion ODEs with the convergence order guarantee. DPM-Solver is suitable for both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs without any further training. Experimental results show that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in only 10 to 20 function evaluations on various datasets. We achieve 4.70 FID in 10 function evaluations and 2.87 FID in 20 function evaluations on the CIFAR10 dataset, and a 4 _∼_ 16 _×_ speedup compared with previous state-of-the-art training-free samplers on various datasets.[2]

## **1 Introduction**

Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) [1–3] are emerging powerful generative models with promising performance on many tasks, such as image generation [4, 5], video generation [6], text-to-image generation [7], speech synthesis [8, 9] and lossless compression [10]. DPMs are defined by discretetime random processes [1, 2] or continuous-time stochastic differential equations (SDEs) [3], which learn to gradually remove the noise added to the data points. Compared with the widely-used generative adversarial networks (GANs) [11] and variational auto-encoders (VAEs) [12], DPMs can not only compute exact likelihood [3], but also achieve even better sample quality for image generation [4]. However, to obtain high-quality samples, DPMs usually need hundreds or thousands of sequential steps of large neural network evaluations, thereby resulting in a much slower sampling speed than the single-step GANs or VAEs. Such inefficiency is becoming a critical bottleneck for the adoption of DPMs in downstream tasks, leading to an urgent request to design fast samplers for DPMs.

Existing fast samplers for DPMs can be divided into two categories. The first category includes knowledge distillation [13, 14] and noise level or sample trajectory learning [15–18]. Such methods require a possibly expensive training stage before they can be used for efficient sampling. Furthermore, their applicability and flexibility might be limited. It might require nontrivial effort to adapt the method to different models, datasets, and number of sampling steps. The second category consists of training-free [19–21] samplers, which are suitable for all pre-trained DPMs in a simple plug-andplay manner. Training-free samplers include adopting implicit [19] or analytical [21] generation process, advanced differential equation (DE) solvers [3, 20, 22–24] and dynamic programming [18]. However, these methods still require _∼_ 50 function evaluations [21] to generate high-quality samples (comparable to those generated by plain samplers in about 1000 function evaluations), thereby are still time-consuming.

In this work, we bring the efficiency of training-free samplers to a new level to produce high-quality samples in the “ _few-step sampling_ ” regime, where the sampling can be done within around 10 steps of sequential function evaluations. We tackle the alternative problem of sampling from DPMs as solving the corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs) of DPMs, and carefully examine the structure of diffusion ODEs. Diffusion ODEs have a semi-linear structure — they consist of a linear function of the data variable and a nonlinear function parameterized by neural networks. Such structure is omitted in previous training-free samplers [3, 20], which directly use black-box DE solvers. To utilize the semi-linear structure, we derive an exact formulation of the solutions of diffusion ODEs by analytically computing the linear part of the solutions, avoiding the corresponding discretization error. Furthermore, by applying change-of-variable, the solutions can be equivalently simplified to an exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Such integral is very special and can be efficiently approximated by the numerical methods for exponential integrators [25].

Based on our formulation of solutions, we propose _DPM-Solver_ , a fast dedicated solver for diffusion ODEs by approximating the above integral. Specifically, we propose first-order, second-order and third-order versions of DPM-Solver with convergence order guarantees. We further propose an adaptive step size schedule for DPM-Solver. In general, DPM-Solver is applicable to both continuoustime and discrete-time DPMs, and also conditional sampling with classifier guidance [4]. Fig. 1 demonstrates the speedup performance of a Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [19] baseline and DPM-Solver, which shows that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples with as few as 10 function evaluations and is much faster than DDIM on the ImageNet 256x256 dataset [26]. Our additional experimental results show that DPM-Solver can greatly improve the sampling speed of both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs, and it can achieve excellent sample quality in around 10 function evaluations, which is much faster than all previous training-free samplers of DPMs.

## **2.2 Diffusion (Probability Flow) ODEs**

When discretizing SDEs, the step size is limited by the randomness of the Wiener process [27, Chap. 11]. A large step size (small number of steps) often causes non-convergence, especially in high dimensional spaces. For faster sampling, one can consider the associated _probability flow ODE_ [3], which has the same marginal distribution at each time _t_ as that of the SDE. Specifically, for DPMs, Song et al. [3] proved that the probability flow ODE of Eq. (2.4) is

Samples can be drawn by solving the ODE from _T_ to 0. Comparing with SDEs, ODEs can be solved with larger step sizes as they have no randomness. Furthermore, we can take advantage of efficient numerical ODE solvers to accelerate the sampling. Song et al. [3] used the RK45 ODE solver [28] for the diffusion ODEs, which generates samples in _∼_ 60 function evaluations to reach comparable quality with a 1000-step SDE solver for Eq. (2.5) on the CIFAR-10 dataset [29]. However, existing _∼_ general-purpose ODE solvers still cannot generate satisfactory samples in the few-step ( 10 steps) sampling regime. To the best of our knowledge, there is still a lack of training-free samplers for DPMs in the few-step sampling regime, and the sampling speed of DPMs is still a critical issue.

## **3 Customized Fast Solvers for Diffusion ODEs**

As highlighted in Sec. 2.2, discretizing SDEs is generally difficult in high dimensions [27, Chap. 11] and it is hard to converge within few steps. In contrast, ODEs are easier to solve, yielding a potential for fast samplers. However, as mentioned in Sec. 2.2, the general black-box ODE solver used in previous work [3] empirically fails to converge in few steps. This motivates us to design a dedicated solver for diffusion ODEs to enable fast and high-quality few-step sampling. We start with a detailed investigation of the specific structure of diffusion ODEs.

## **3.3 Step Size Schedule**

The proposed solvers in Sec. 3.2 need to specify the time steps _{ti}[M] i_ =0[in advance.][We propose two] choices of the time step schedule. One choice is handcrafted, which is to uniformly split the interval [ _λT_ , _λ_ 0], i.e. _λti_ = _λT_ + _Mi_[(] _[λ]_[0] _[−][λ][T]_[ )][,] _[i]_[=][0] _[, . . . , M]_[.][Note that this is different from previous] work [2, 3] which chooses uniform steps for _ti_ . Empirically, DPM-Solver with uniform time steps _λti_ can already generate quite good samples in few steps, where results are listed in Appendix E. As the other choice, we propose an adaptive step size algorithm, which dynamically adjusts the step size by combining different orders of DPM-Solver. The adaptive algorithm is inspired by [20] and we defer its implementation details to Appendix C.

For few-step sampling, we need to use up all the number of function evaluations (NFE). When the NFE is not divisible by 3, we firstly apply DPM-Solver-3 as much as possible, and then add a single step of DPM-Solver-1 or DPM-Solver-2 (dependent on the reminder of _K_ divided by 3), as detailed in Appendix D. In the subsequent experiments, we use such combination of solvers with the uniform step size schedule for NFE _≤_ 20, and otherwise the adaptive step size schedule.

## **4 Comparison with Existing Fast Sampling Methods**

Here, we discuss the relationship and highlight the difference between DPM-Solver and existing ODE-based fast sampling methods for DPMs. We further briefly discuss the advantage of training-free samplers over those training-based ones.

## **4.1 DDIM as DPM-Solver-1**

Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [19] design a deterministic method for fast sampling˜ from DPMs. For two adjacent time steps _ti−_ 1 and _ti_ , assume that we have a solution _**x** ti−_ 1 at time _ti−_ 1, then a single step of DDIM from time _ti−_ 1 to time _ti_ is

(3.7). However, the semi-linear ODE formulation of DPM-Solver allows for principled generalization to higher-order solvers and convergence order analysis.

Recent work [13] also show that DDIM is a first-order discretization of diffusion ODEs by differentiating both sides of Eq. (4.1). However, they cannot explain the difference between DDIM and the first-order Euler discretization of diffusion ODEs. In contrast, by showing that DDIM is a special case of DPM-Solver, we reveal that DDIM makes full use of the semi-linearity of diffusion ODEs, which explains its superiority over traditional Euler methods.

The approximation error of explicit RK methods depends on _**h** θ_ , which consists of the error corresponding to both the linear term _f_ ( _τ_ ) _**x** τ_ and the nonlinear noise prediction model _**ϵ** θ_ . However, the error of the linear term may increase exponentially because the exact solution of the linear term has an exponential coefficient (as shown in Eq. (3.1)). There are many empirical evidence [25, 31] showing that directly using explicit RK methods for semi-linear ODEs may suffer from unstable numerical issues for large step size. We also demonstrate the empirical difference of the proposed DPM-Solver and the traditional explicit RK methods in Sec. 5.1, which shows that DPM-Solver have smaller discretization errors than the RK methods with the same order.

## **4.3 Training-based Fast Sampling Methods for DPMs**

Samplers that need extra training or optimization include knowledge distillation [13, 14], learning the noise level or variance [15, 16, 33], and learning the noise schedule or sample trajectory [17, 18]. Although the progressive distillation method [13] can obtain a fast sampler within 4 steps, it needs further training costs and loses part of the information in the original DPM (e.g., after distillation, the noise prediction model cannot predict the noise (score function) at every time step between [0 _, T_ ]). In contrast, training-free samplers can keep all the information of the original model, and thereby can be directly extended to the conditional sampling by combining the original model and an external classifier [4] (e.g. see Appendix D for the conditional sampling with classifier guidance).

Beyond directly designing fast samplers for DPMs, several works also propose novel types of DPMs which supports faster sampling. For instance, defining a low-dimensional latent variable for DPMs [34]; designing special diffusion processes with bounded score functions [35]; combining GANs with the reverse process of DPMs [36]. The proposed DPM-Solver may also be suitable for accelerating the sampling of these DPMs, and we leave them for future work.

## **5 Experiments**

In this section, we show that as a training-free sampler, DPM-Solver can greatly speedup the sampling of existing pre-trained DPMs, including both continuous-time and discrete-time ones, with both linear noise schedule [2, 19] and cosine noise schedule [16]. We vary different number of function evaluations (NFE) which is the number of calls to the noise prediction model _**ϵ** θ_ ( _**x** t, t_ ), and compare the sample quality between DPM-Solver and other methods. For each experiment, We draw 50K samples and use the widely adopted FID score [37] to evaluate the sample quality, where lower FID usually implies better sample quality.

Unless explicitly mentioned, we always use the solver combination with the uniform step size schedule in Sec. 3.3 if the NFE budget is less than 20, and otherwise the DPM-Solver-3 with the adaptive step size schedule in Sec. 3.3. We refer to Appendix D for other implementation details of DPM-Solver and Appendix E for detailed settings.

## **5.1 Comparison with Continuous-Time Sampling Methods**

We firstly compare DPM-Solver with other continuous-time sampling methods for DPMs. The compared methods include the Euler-Maruyama discretization for diffusion SDEs [3], the adaptive step size solver for diffusion SDEs [20] and the RK methods for diffusion ODEs [3, 28] in Eq. (2.7). We compare these methods for sampling from a pre-trained continuous-time “VP deep” model [3] on the CIFAR-10 dataset [29] with the linear noise schedule.

Fig. 2a shows the efficiency of compared solvers. We use uniform time steps with 50, 200, 1000 NFE for the diffusion SDE with Euler discretization, and vary the tolerance hyperparameter [3, 20] for the adaptive step size SDE solver [20] and RK45 ODE solver [28] to control the NFE. DPM-Solver can generate good sample quality within around 10 NFE, while other solvers have large discretization error even in 50 NFE, which shows that DPM-Solver can achieve _∼_ 5 speedup of the previous best solver. In particular, we achieve 4.70 FID with 10 NFE, 3.75 FID with 12 NFE, 3.24 FID with 15 NFE, and 2.87 FID with 20 NFE, which is the fastest sampler on CIFAR-10.

As an ablation study, we also compare the second-order and third-order DPM-Solver and RK methods, as shown in Table 1. We compare RK methods for diffusion ODEs w.r.t. both time _t_ in Eq. (2.7) and half-log-SNR _λ_ by applying change-of-variable (see detailed formulations in Appendix E.1). The results show that given the same NFE, the sample quality of DPM-Solver is consistently better than RK methods with the same order. The superior efficiency of DPM-Solver is particularly evident in the few-step regime under 15 NFE, where RK methods have rather large discretization errors. This is mainly because DPM-Solver analytically computes the linear term, avoiding the corresponding discretization error. Besides, the higher order DPM-Solver-3 converges faster than DPM-Solver-2, which matches the order analysis in Theorem 3.2.

## **5.2 Comparison with Discrete-Time Sampling Methods**

We use the method in Sec. 3.4 for using DPM-Solver in discrete-time DPMs, and then compare DPM-Solver with other discrete-time training-free samplers, including DDPM [2], DDIM [19], Analytic-DDPM [21], Analytic-DDIM [21], PNDM [22], FastDPM [38] and Itô-Taylor [24]. We also compare with GGDM [18], which uses the same pre-trained model but needs further training for the sampling trajectory. We compare the sample quality by varying NFE from 10 to 1000.

9

Specifically, we use the discrete-time model trained by _L_ simple in [2] on the CIFAR-10 dataset with linear noise schedule; the discrete-time model in [19] on CelebA 64x64 [39] with linear noise schedule; the discrete-time model trained by _L_ hybrid in [16] on ImageNet 64x64 [26] with cosine noise schedule; the discrete-time model with classifier guidance in [4] on ImageNet 128x128 [26] with linear noise schedule; the discrete-time model in [4] on LSUN bedroom 256x256 [40] with linear noise schedule. For the models trained on ImageNet, we only use their “mean” model and omit the “variance” model. As shown in Fig. 2, on all datasets, DPM-Solver can obtain reasonable samples within 12 steps (FID 4.65 on CIFAR-10, FID 3.71 on CelebA 64x64 and FID 19.97 on ImageNet 64x64, FID 4.08 on ImageNet 128x128), which is 4 _∼_ 16 _×_ faster than the previous fastest training-free sampler. DPM-Solver even outperforms GGDM, which requires additional training.

## **6 Conclusions**

We tackle the problem of fast and training-free sampling from DPMs. We propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated training-free solver of diffusion ODEs for fast sampling of DPMs in around 10 steps of function evaluations. DPM-Solver leverages the semi-linearity of diffusion ODEs and it directly approximates a simplified formulation of exact solutions of diffusion ODEs, which consists of an exponentially weighted integral of the noise prediction model. Inspired by numerical methods for exponential integrators, we propose first-order, second-order and third-order DPMSolver to approximate the exponentially weighted integral of noise prediction models with theoretical convergence guarantee. We propose both handcrafted and adaptive step size schedule, and apply DPM-Solver for both continuous-time and discrete-time DPMs. Our experimental results show that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in around 10 function evaluations on various datasets, and it can achieve 4 _∼_ 16 _×_ speedup compared with previous state-of-the-art training-free samplers.

**Limitations and broader impact** Despite the promising speedup performance, DPM-Solver is designed for fast sampling, which may be not suitable for accelerating the likelihood evaluations of DPMs. Besides, compared to the commonly-used GANs, diffusion models with DPM-Solver are still not fast enough for real-time applications. In addition, like other deep generative models, DPMs may be used to generate adverse fake contents, and the proposed solver may further amplify the potential undesirable influence of deep generative models for malicious applications.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is an exemplary piece of technical academic writing, characterized by a highly professional, structured, and goal-oriented narrative. The quality is exceptional: the authors effectively bridge a complex theoretical framework—the semi-linear structure of diffusion ODEs—with a practical contribution that addresses a significant bottleneck in generative modeling. The clarity is commendable, particularly in the introduction, which expertly contextualizes the research gap between computationally demanding "black-box" solvers and the specific requirements of the "few-step sampling" regime. The writing is precise, well-motivated, and avoids unnecessary jargon, making the transition from theoretical derivation to experimental validation seamless. The most significant strength of the work is this "problem-method-result" alignment; by identifying that previous solvers ignored the linear structure of diffusion ODEs, the authors provide a logically sound justification for their proposed exponential integrator-based approach. There are no glaring issues to report from this opening; the document effectively positions itself as both a foundational theoretical advancement for diffusion processes and a highly impactful practical utility for the machine learning community.

Without explicit definition of the resulting coefficients (which are standard in stiff solvers but non-trivial here), a reader might struggle to reproduce the exact update rules in Algorithms 1 and 2, which rely on these specific analytical solutions. While Appendix B.2 is cited as the source, the main text's claim that this is 'analytically computed' lacks the necessary detail to distinguish it from a standard RK derivation, given the specific non-linear weight $e^{-\lambda}$.

comments.title: Logical gap in Step Size Schedule for few-step sampling
comments.quote: When the NFE is not divisible by 3, we firstly apply DPM-Solver-3 as much as possible, and then add a single step of DPM-Solver-1 or DPM-Solver-2

If NFE is, for example, 4, the instruction says to use DPM-Solver-3 (3 calls) then a solver for the remaining 1 NFE (DPM-Solver-1). This is efficient, but the text lacks a condition or justification for why order consistency (using a lower-order solver at the end of the trajectory) does not degrade the C-order convergence guarantee established in Theorem 3.2. While locally this is a common heuristic in ODE solving, the transition from high-order to low-order steps is a potential source of error accumulation that is not theoretically addressed.

comments.title: Inconsistent Taylor expansion order notation
comments.quote: For $k \ge 1$, the $(k - 1)$-th order Taylor expansion of $\hat{\epsilon}_\theta(\hat{x}_\lambda, \lambda)$ w.r.t. $\lambda$... Substituting the above Taylor expansion into Eq. (3.5) yields: ... $x_{ti} \approx (\alpha_{ti} / \alpha_{ti-1}) * x_{ti-1} - \alpha_{ti} * \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} [ \dots ] * \epsilon_\theta^{[n]}(\dots)$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors define $k$ as the order of the solver and state that the $(k-1)$-th order Taylor expansion is used. However, the summation in Equation (3.6) ranges from $n=0$ to $k-1$. A Taylor expansion of degree $d$ (which results in a $d$-th order error term) typically involves terms up to the $d$-th derivative, which would be $n=0$ to $d$. Specifically, a $k$-th order solver should ideally utilize derivatives up to $k-1$ in the expansion to achieve $O(h^k)$ convergence, but the indexing is slightly ambiguous here. While $n=k-1$ matches the $(k-1)$-th order expansion mentioned, it is standard in numerical integration to use higher-order derivatives for higher-order convergence. The internal consistency between 'k-th order solver' and 'summation to k-1' is technically correct for the first-order approximation, but the jump to $k=2, 3$ implicitly relies on the stiff order conditions rather than a simple Taylor series. The text is slightly confusing whether the summation limit is intended to define the accuracy or if it is a rigid definition of the solver.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated approach to accelerating sampling in diffusion probabilistic models by leveraging the semi-linear structure of the associated ODEs. The writing is clear, with a strong introductory narrative that effectively positions the work relative to existing training-free and distillation-based methods. The core idea—analytically solving the linear component of the diffusion ODE and applying exponential integrators for the nonlinear part—is innovative and leads to impressive practical gains (4.70 FID in 10 NFE on CIFAR10). However, there are notable issues to address. First, the claim of an "exact formulation of the solution" is somewhat misleading, as only the linear part is handled exactly; the solution ultimately still requires approximation of the nonlinear integral, a point that should be clarified early. Second, while the paper promises convergence order guarantees, the provided excerpt does not include any theoretical analysis or empirical validation of these orders, leaving a significant gap in the current exposition. Finally, the practical robustness of the method—particularly regarding step-size scheduling and numerical stability across diverse noise schedules and guidance strengths—is not yet discussed, but will be critical for adoption. Overall, the paper is of high quality and clarity, but needs to temper its claims and provide more rigorous support for its theoretical contributions.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of λ_0 in step size schedule

comments.explanation: The authors define λ_t = log(α_t/σ_t). At time t=0, α_0=1 and σ_0=0, so λ_0 = log(1/0) → +∞, which is not a finite real number. This makes the interval [λ_T, λ_0] ill-defined and the uniform splitting formula meaningless. The authors likely intend to use a small positive time ε (e.g., the final discrete time step) instead of 0, but this is not stated. Without clarification, a reader attempting to implement the method cannot determine the endpoint. This is a real mathematical inconsistency, not just a notational oversight.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper introduces DPM-Solver, a training-free high-order ODE solver that dramatically reduces the sampling cost of diffusion probabilistic models to approximately 10 function evaluations without retraining, addressing a critical efficiency bottleneck with significant practical impact. The introduction is exceptionally clear, effectively motivating the work by contrasting costly distillation methods and suboptimal black-box samplers, and the core technical insight—analytically resolving the linear component of the semi-linear diffusion ODE to reformulate the problem as an exponentially weighted integral—appears theoretically robust and well-motivated. The abstract highlights impressive empirical results, including competitive FID scores on CIFAR-10 and 4–16× speedups over baselines, underscoring the method's effectiveness. However, a significant issue to scrutinize is the novelty assessment: the work must clearly delineate its contributions beyond the application of established exponential integrator theory, particularly verifying that the adaptive step-size schedules and convergence guarantees are non-trivial and specifically tailored to the unique dynamics of DPMs. Additionally, while the provided text exhibits high writing quality, precise notation, and strong preliminary formulations, the abrupt cutoff in Section 2 precludes a full evaluation of the mathematical rigor and experimental breadth, necessitating a check of the remaining manuscript for complete proofs and comprehensive comparisons against recent distillation-based alternatives. Overall, the paper is timely, well-written, and shows high potential, contingent on the technical depth of the full derivations.

comments.title: Imprecise terminology regarding 'stiff order conditions'
comments.quote: By dropping the O(h_i^{k+1}) error term and approximating the first (k-1)-th total derivatives with the “stiff order conditions” [31, 32], we can derive k-th-order ODE solvers for diffusion ODEs.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the derivatives are approximated 'with the stiff order conditions.' In the literature of exponential integrators (e.g., Hochbruck & Ostermann), stiff order conditions are algebraic constraints used to *derive* the weights and stages of a Runge-Kutta method to guarantee a specific convergence order. They are not a technique for *approximating* derivatives. The derivatives \hat{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_\theta^{[n]} are actually approximated by evaluating the noise predictor at the intermediate points \mathbf{u}_i (via finite differences or direct function evaluation). The phrasing conflates the derivation criterion with the approximation mechanism. While the citation clarifies the intent, the sentence is terminologically imprecise and could mislead readers unfamiliar with exponential integrator theory.

comments.title: Missing explicit smoothness assumptions in convergence theorem
comments.quote: Assume _**ϵ** θ_ ( _**x** t, t_ ) _follows the regularity condiODEs, i.e., for the sequencetions detailed in Appendix B.1, then for {_ _**x**_ ˜ _ti}[M] i_ =1 _[computed by DPM-Solver-] k_ = 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 _, DPM-Solver[k] k[, the approximation error at time] is a k-th order solver for diffusion_[ 0] ˜ _satisfies_ _**x** tM −_ _**x**_ 0 = _O_ ( _h[k]_ max[)] _[, where][ h][max]_[= max][1] _[≤][i][≤][M]_[(] _[λ][t] i[−][λ][t] i−_ 1[)] _[.]_
comments.explanation: Theorem 3.2 claims global $k$-th order convergence but only references Appendix B.1 for regularity conditions. For Taylor-based exponential integrators to achieve order $k$, the vector field (here, the noise predictor \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta) must be $k$-times continuously differentiable with respect to \mathbf{x} and \lambda, with bounded derivatives. The theorem statement itself does not explicitly specify these smoothness requirements. Without them, the $O(h_{\max}^k)$ global error bound does not strictly hold. While deferring to an appendix is common, a convergence theorem should briefly state the necessary differentiability class of \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta in the main text to ensure the claim is self-contained and mathematically complete.

Assuming the most plausible author intent based on standard DDPM literature, the text should read: 'Discrete-time DPMs [2] train the noise prediction model at N fixed time steps {t_n}_{n=1}^N... where each x_n corresponds to the value at time t_n. We can transform the discrete-time noise prediction model to the continuous version by letting \tilde{\epsilon}_\theta(x, t) := \epsilon_\theta(x, (N-1)t)... Note that the input time of \tilde{\epsilon}_\theta may not be integers, but we find that the noise prediction model can still work well, and we hypothesize that it is because of the smooth time embeddings...' The underlying mathematical mapping (linear scaling of discrete indices to [0,1]) is standard and correct. progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a promising approach to addressing the critical bottleneck of sampling speed in Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs), proposing DPM-Solver to achieve high-quality generation in just 10 to 20 steps. The writing is clear and effectively motivates the work by contrasting the efficiency gap of DPMs with GANs/VAEs and critiquing the limitations of existing training-free samplers, which still require 50+ steps. The primary strength of the manuscript lies in its theoretical contribution: the authors claim to leverage the semi-linear structure of diffusion ODEs to derive an exact analytic solution that outperforms standard black-box ODE solvers. However, the most significant issue for this review is the mathematical rigor of the proposed exact formulation. Since the paper asserts that this formulation avoids discretization errors inherent in previous methods, the derivation of the "exponentially weighted integral" approximation must be presented with high precision. If the proof of convergence order is flawed or the approximation introduces subtle bias, the paper’s claim to be a "dedicated" solver rather than simply an improved differential equation solver would be undermined. Furthermore, while the introduction successfully frames the problem, the transition from the general SDE definition in Section 2 to the specific integral approximation in the methodology section requires careful execution to ensure the clarity of the contribution.

comments.explanation: Merged from Issues 1 and 3. The text renders the transition distribution incorrectly, appearing as a formatting artifact like `_q_ 0 _t_` or a LaTeX/OCR error `$q_0 t$`. The standard notation for the forward diffusion process is $q_t(x_t|x_0)$, and the presence of an explicit '0' before 't' suggests a typesetting or spacing error (likely a misinterpretation of subscript braces).

comments.title: Algorithmic notation error in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: $\alpha\alpha_{t_i-1}$
comments.explanation: The provided text renders $\alpha\alpha_{t_i-1}$ where the intended formula in the DPM-Solver algorithm is likely a ratio of noise schedule coefficients (e.g., $\frac{\alpha_{t_i}}{\alpha_{t_{i-1}}}$). The lack of a slash or separator between the alphas makes the notation syntactically incorrect and impossible to interpret as a mathematical operation.

comments.title: Ambiguous inverse function definition
comments.quote: satisfying $t \iff \lambda$
comments.explanation: The phrase 'satisfying $t \iff \lambda$' is non-standard notation for a function inverse. While it suggests a bijective relationship, a more precise description would be 'satisfying $t_{\lambda}(\lambda(t)) = t$' or 'where $t$ is the inverse function of $\lambda$'. This could mislead a reader unfamiliar with the notation regarding the specific mapping between time and the log-SNR variable.

comments.title: Incomplete LaTeX formula in Proposition 3.1
comments.quote: $\int e^{−\lambda} \hat{\epsilon}_\theta ( \hat{x}_\lambda, \lambda ) d\lambda$
comments.explanation: The LaTeX definition of the integral in Proposition 3.1 is broken at the end (indicated by a stray bracket `]` or incomplete syntax). Additionally, the integral limits (e.g., from $\lambda_s$ to $\lambda_t$) appear to be missing, rendering the formula incomplete.

comments.title: Typo in the description of step composition
comments.quote: dependent on the reminder of K divided by 3
comments.explanation: The phrase 'dependent on the reminder of K divided by 3' uses 'reminder' which is a homophone error for 'remainder'. While the intent is clearly defined (which solver to use based on the integer division of the total steps), this is a direct spelling mistake.

comments.title: Inconsistent experimental setup description between context and passage
comments.quote: otherwise the DPM-Solver-3 with the adaptive step size schedule
comments.explanation: The passage introduces a specific solver 'DPM-Solver-3' associated with the adaptive step size schedule for NFE > 20. However, the context provided for Section 3.3 states simply 'and otherwise the adaptive step size schedule' without specifying a solver order. This contradiction creates ambiguity about the reproducibility of the 'subsequent experiments' described in this section.

comments.title: Gibberish formula in continuous-time transformation

The preceding phrase 'lettinginput' is also a typo. While the intent to describe reparameterization is clear, the actual mathematical formulation is lost, making it impossible to verify or implement from this text alone.

comments.title: Duplicate Reference Citation
comments.quote: DDPM [2], DDIM [19], Analytic-DDPM [21], Analytic-DDIM [21], PNDM [22], FastDPM [38] and Itô-Taylor [24]
comments.explanation: The authors list baselines to compare against and cite `Analytic-DDPM [21]` and `Analytic-DDIM [21]` with the same reference number. This appears to be a citation indexing error, likely intending to distinguish between the two distinct papers (Lu et al. and Luo et al.) or intending to omit the duplicate. It confuses the reader regarding the provenance of the method 'Analytic-DDIM'.

comments.title: Ambiguous Value for 12 Function Evaluations
comments.quote: we achieve 4.70 FID with 10 NFE, 3.75 FID with 12 NFE... within 12 steps (FID 4.65 on CIFAR-10...)
comments.explanation: The paper reports a FID of 3.75 at 12 NFE in the context of the continuous-time 'VP deep' model (Section 5.1), and a FID of 4.65 'within 12 steps' for the discrete-time models (Section 5.2). While these may correspond to different experiments (continuous vs. discrete), the phrasing is confusing. 'Within 12 steps' typically implies 12 NFE. If the author meant '12 NFE', there is a contradiction between 3.75 and 4.65. Even if the numbers are correct for different models, the language could be clearer to avoid implying a discrepancy.

comments.title: Typos in Describing Model Training
comments.quote: the discrete-time model trained by _L_ simple in [2] on the CIFAR-10 dataset
comments.explanation: The text uses the subscript `_L_` (e.g., `_L_ simple` and `_L_ hybrid`). Given the surrounding context and the usage in other papers of this era (e.g., 'a simple' noise schedule), this is likely a typo for 'a simple' or 'a hybrid'. It introduces minor ambiguity as 'L' is not a defined variable for model type in the provided context, though it is easily inferable.
